title: SHARP ECHOES
text: SHARP ECHOES by Kelli Owen "Shit.” Erin sighed as the elevator doors slid open to reveal an empty hallway. She froze, her scars triggered, her fears alive. I’m not ready for this. It had been almost a year since the attack. She was healing but not healed. And while Erin had always felt empathy for survivors of trauma, she had never fully understood the damage until becoming one herself. The elevator doors closed and the small space felt both safe and suffocating. Her heart sunk as she stood unmoving and became hostage to a mental barrage of unwanted memories. After the assault, Erin had used her remaining vacation days to stay home, or rather, to hide at home. She tried to process the attack on her own, to adjust to a new her, but ended up alternating between scalding showers of manic rage and prolonged naps of overwhelming depression. She had broken more than she’d fixed. Erin knew she was sinking but feared the stigma of being seen as a victim, so she sought help in another town. The lights of the elevator flickered overhead and Erin clearly remembered Doctor Mason’s office, with its smell of citrus essential oils and the supple texture of leather furniture. Caught somewhere between a ’70s cliché and new age therapy, it had turned out to be exactly what Erin needed. Erin’s reactions were welcomed and worked through with a soft voice and kind smile. There were exercises and prescriptions—treating her highs and lows until she finally evened out. And when her emotions were balanced, the doctor suggested flooding to break through what had become an almost obsessive fear, a phobia—the parking garage at work. Her breath caught at the idea of the airy four-story garage, and Erin reached over to steady herself on the elevator’s handrail. I’ve come too far to fail here. Erin inhaled through her nose and exhaled out her mouth, releasing the air slowly through pursed lips. She remembered the work she’d done, the progress she’d made. In the nose, out the mouth, three more times as she tried to remember her healing rather than her trauma. Once her allotted time-off had been depleted, Erin returned to work but not her parking spot. Rather than using the attached garage, available to employees and customers of the York Towers office building, Erin parked in the lot down the block. She then walked through the front door of the office building to ride the elevator to the fourth floor. Rain or shine, and through several frigid winter days, she’d gone the extra mile to not use the parking garage. Doctor Mason began breaking down that wall the moment Erin had revealed her new habit. “Flooding is a technique in which you immerse yourself in your fear in a safe way.” The doctor had explained the behavior therapy procedure. “Considering your situation, we can do it in stages. First things first, we have to get you out of the cold.” Erin moved her car to the ground floor of the garage, but parked as close to the entrance as possible and all but running back out to the safety of the sidewalk rather than walking through the garage itself. She then continued to the front door of the building as she had been, but was proud of her baby step of actually being within the cement prison, even if only briefly. After a month, the doctor pushed Erin to the next step, her parking space. But Erin couldn’t do it. She couldn’t be on that floor. Erin imagined little things she had walked past every day without noticing and expected they would jump out and scream her location, remind her of exactly where she’d been when it had happened. Erin settled for the second level of the parking garage, the floor with the skywalk, knowing it was a hub and should be occupied at all times. She made sure to arrive and leave when the bulk of the building’s employees did, so she wouldn’t be walking alone through the echoing tomb to and from the skywalk. Her knuckles whitened as Erin gripped the railing in the elevator, eyes aimed at the floor but focused in the past. She felt the weight of depression creeping in as she relived months of therapy, but fought the tendrils of fear threatening to drag her down. She inhaled deeply, counted to three, and exhaled. I can do this. Erin remembered the hard work of processing and conquering her anger, even as she dug inside to find it again and tap it for strength, telling herself, Just a little. Erin had been successfully using the second level of the parking garage for a little over a month and had almost returned to her pre-attack emotional indifference to the space within the cement walls. Parking next to Gloria had been an accident the first time, but the woman was a familiar face to Erin and brought comfort. The owner-operator of the second floor Starbucks, Gloria was the perfect companion to walk beside, and Erin quickly made it a habit. The coffee nook was positioned beside the elevators, and Erin had begun to convince herself their twice-daily meetings were a friendship rather than conveniently linked to the garage or flooding of her fear. Gloria. Erin pushed the button marked L2 and the doors opened again immediately—not called elsewhere by other passengers, the elevator had simply waited for her to decide where to go. She poked her head out. The Starbucks was dark. “Shit.” Erin hadn’t meant to stay so late. She’d lost track of time, working diligently with her project spread out on the long conference room table. If she’d been at her own desk, near windows, she would have noticed the setting sun. She would have known to leave. Now the building was all but empty and promised the same of its attached parking garage. I can do this. She swallowed hard, exhaled shakily, and stepped from the elevator. Her pounding heart felt as if it was going to break through her ribs, and Erin put a hand to her chest as if to prevent its escape. She jumped as the elevator doors hissed closed behind her. And though Erin knew she could push the button and go back in, she held her ground, resolute in the decision to continue. Retreat would be failure. Acutely aware of her surroundings, Erin walked toward the exterior metal door, which led to the skywalk and the garage beyond. She had only gone alone into the garage twice, and both times had been midday, under a bright sun. Not at night. Never at night. She paused in front of the Starbucks. Why didn’t Gloria wait for me? For a moment, Erin felt the pang of abandonment, but quickly shook the notion away. She knew Gloria didn’t know of her struggle, and the barista wasn’t responsible for Erin’s healing. Erin considered heading down to the first floor and using the front door. The ground floor boasted a spacious patio area in the center, surrounded by several small food stands and an all-night copy center. It was well lit and likely occupied. She wouldn’t be alone. But she would still need to walk around to the parking garage and go up to the second level. She would need to walk all the way to the back wall where she had bravely followed Gloria’s parking lead that morning. I’m a grown woman. I cannot stand here and cower all night. She hissed rather than exhaled, determination finding its way past her worry. Erin dug into her shoulder bag and retrieved her keys, gave the elevators and dark coffee nook one more glance, and then turned toward the door. Erin took several quick strides to reach the exterior door and pushed it with more force than necessary, as if she were proving her willpower and inner strength with the show of exaggerated confidence. Eyes forward, she hurried through the skywalk without looking at the street below or the darkening sky above. She burst through the metal door with vigor but froze in the night air beyond. The garage, mottled gray with bright repair patches on the walls and dark vehicle fluid stains on the ground, was industrialized tie-dye. And it was empty. A handful of parked cars dotted the open expanse, but no other living soul occupied the level. The overhead lights buzzed and Erin’s shoulders tightened, as if the sound had physically touched her. Erin couldn’t see her car behind the elevator block, which was positioned in the center of the garage. But she knew exactly where she’d parked and headed toward it. Barely a dozen yards in and her mouth went dry. The small sounds of the garage were tiny punctuation marks to her dark thoughts. Erin imagined the rustle of paper was the casual shuffling of a perpetrator’s quiet shoes. The flicker of the lights moved shadows and gave them life, breath. She stopped and looked around. The doctor’s wisdom crawled into her mind and Erin whispered to the quiet garage, “Assess the truth.” The first time she had heard the phrase, Erin had been in the midst of a full-blown breakdown, slumped to the floor of the doctor’s office, knees pulled tight to her chest, crying with such anguish as she tried to discuss the rape. “Assess the truth.” The therapist had joined Erin on the floor and gently held her shoulders. “You need to calm down and remember. Don’t let your brain panic and fill in details that aren’t true. Don’t presume or conflate. Examine what really happened.” “But I remember it. He pushed up my skirt and—.” “No. No, he did not.” Doctor Mason had paused for what felt like hours, watching Erin. Erin shook her head, and the doctor finally pushed the truth forward for Erin. “You were attacked. You were mugged. But you were not sexually assaulted. I’ve read the file. You’ve read the file. You weren’t even wearing a skirt that day. You were—” “I had on my blue pantsuit. I hated that pantsuit.” The details cleared, and as Erin sat on the expensive rug in the doctor’s office, she started to remember the actual attack. It had been violent, but not a violation. It had been traumatic, but it had been so on its own merit, and Erin didn’t need to make it any worse or deepen the stains it would already leave. Her life had been threatened, but not her honor. Erin looked around again and assessed her reality, grounding herself in logic. Business hours were over, so the garage was empty. The lights always flickered—this was neither new nor foreboding. The shadows were just that—shadows. And her car was up ahead, albeit behind the elevator bank. I was attacked, but I survived. Hell, I survived therapy. I can survive an empty garage. Erin continued, her pace still slow and cautious, but steady. After several moments, Erin realized she was flinching with each step, not from trepidation but as a reaction to her own movements. The sharp echoes of her high heels were like a constant tap of anticipation. The slow dripping of a leaking sink keeping you awake at night. The ticking of a clock your insomnia hugs for company. Erin reached down and removed her heels, carrying them together in her left hand, her right already defensively gripping her keys, the metal protruding between her fingers. With her steps silent, her thoughts could scream and echo in the cavity of residual pain where her fears remained alive. Erin hadn’t been raped, but she had been assaulted. His initial attack had been as he stepped from between the cars and simply appeared in front of Erin, pushing her unexpectedly. Erin had lost her footing and went down, hard, hitting her tailbone on the unforgiving cement floor of the garage. He yanked her purse from her grip, and when Erin reached up reflexively to stop him, he grabbed her arm and twisted it, forcing her to spin around. He pushed, leveraging her shoulder into the cold concrete, and lowered himself, straddling Erin. He leaned in and whispered, the whiskey fumes poisoning the air between them. “Stay down. If you move, if you scream, I will kill you and put you in your own trunk and they won’t find you for days.” He licked her ear, and Erin whimpered. Her saliva-moistened flesh chilled in the night air, as Erin’s mouth became hot and bile threatened the back of her throat. She squeezed her eyes shut, her lip quivering, tears building behind her lids. “Do you understand?” His raspy words filled her mind with images Erin would dream about for months. “You’ll bloat. You’ll begin to rot. The smell will be what finally alerts them, and by then you won’t even be recognizable.” Time stopped. The air felt heavy and her ears rang with the pounding of her pulse. The weight finally lifted from her back and the pressure on her arm subsided. Heavy footsteps ran away, echoing the path of his escape. Erin began counting, eyes still closed, in an attempt to calm her heart and mind, to level out and pull herself together. Only when the cool evening air rushed up her pant leg did Erin feel the chill of embarrassment and realize she’d wet herself. Erin was found—facedown in a fetal position, still counting—by the third-floor lawyer who had parked next to her. The sound of the double-beep as he unlocked his car on approach made Erin break her silence and whimper, which got his attention. The rest of the evening was a blur of worried faces and information Erin wouldn’t have remembered without the discharge instructions. She vaguely recalled her pathetic description to the police. “Taller than me, dark hair, dark eyes, a really straight nose, and smelled of cheap whiskey.” Why did I think his straight nose was so important? Why did I even notice it? Erin had discussed both questions with her therapist, but still wondered about them. The police never caught him. She wasn’t surprised, but it did worry Erin, even though they had reassured her a second attack by the same perpetrator was as likely as being struck by lightning twice. Almost impossible. Erin turned to her left and considered the two cars against the exterior wall. It was the location she had been parked that fateful night, though on the fourth level. She paused and judged the distance to the elevator, honestly assessing the situation almost a year later. Erin shook her head. How had I been so unaware? Careful not to blame herself, Erin pushed away the questions she knew were dangerous—the questions that could bring on depression. No, when faced with the choice, Erin much preferred the strength she found tapping into the anger she wasn’t supposed to hold. Not a lot, not enough to land her back on medication, but just a scratch along the surface. Enough to remind Erin she had survived. But being in the garage alone was feeding the anger tiny morsels of rage, justified and pure. Erin was reliving the smell of whiskey and the foulness of the breath that had bore down on her. Her arm and shoulder throbbed at the memory of the pain, which earned her a sling for several weeks—the mental damage lasted so much longer. Erin recalled the embarrassment several hours later as police spoke to her at the Emergency Room, under lights bright enough to show off the wet spot where she’d pissed herself. For a moment she had believed the man was going to kill her—that she was truly going to die—and her fear had overridden her bladder control. Erin didn’t comprehend until much later, if she hadn’t made a noise, the lawyer may well have run her over, and Erin would have survived the mugging only to be murdered by her catatonia. Erin stared at the shadow between the two cars where her attacker had hidden and listened to her heartbeat in her ears. She didn’t hear the elevator ding behind her. She didn’t notice the door open. And when the man stepped forward, politely offering an evening salutation, Erin spun on instinct. Her tapped rage flooded Erin’s body, her eyes blurred, and her arm shot straight out. She would not be attacked again. She would not become a repeat victim. She would not cower below an aggressor or tremble in fear from empty threats. He would not win. The heel of her shoe made a wet sound as it punctured the man’s eye. The slick suctioning noise, which followed as Erin pulled the shoe back out, was muffled by her primal scream. Her eyes widened, her teeth bared, Erin rammed the shoes forward and drove the heel home a second time. As the man fell backwards, one shoe sticking out from his face, Erin’s hands opened and went to her mouth to cover her scream. The other shoe, and the keys she’d been holding, clattered to the ground. Erin peered down at the man. Her attacker. Adrenaline tasted sour in her mouth. Her heart pounded, a twin staccato to her breathing. Blood ran from his eyes, gravity drawing it down the side of his face to pool in his ear, and backward to soak into his blond hairline. Blond. Erin blinked and relived her final strike. Her arm had gone straight out in front of her. This man was her height, without shoes. Her mugger had been taller than she was in heels. He’d had dark hair, not blond. Erin noted the fallen man’s nose. “Oh no no no.” This was all wrong. This wasn’t her mugger. This wasn’t survival. This wasn’t revenge. This is murd— Erin wouldn’t allow herself to think the word. It wasn’t that. It was self-defense. He came out of the elevator— She paused and glanced at the doors as they slid shut. Or did I? She nodded slowly. Yes, that’s what happened. When I came out of the elevator, he was waiting for me. He ambushed me. She shook her head. No, that’s not right. Erin’s thoughts stopped making sense. Spinning to try and piece together reality, they had created a vortex of nothing and she swam in the silence of a blank slate for several minutes. But it could be what happened. She searched the parking garage again and saw it was still empty of people. She studied York Towers and squinted at the bank of dark office windows. Erin’s gaze returned to the body sprawled outside the elevator. She saw his pants were wet, and she registered the smell of urine in the night air. What have I done? The man wore a casual suit with a crooked tie. This was no lawyer. This was no criminal. This was just some guy at the end of his workday, heading home. Home. Oh God, what if he had a family? What if I took away a father? Erin bent down, unsure if she should check for a pulse. Could he even survive—? The scent of beer drifted up to her. Erin stood, the sadness and grief and empathy previously threatening to pull her into a guilty depression were met with a wall of sudden calm. Not anger, not rage, but a stoic peace. Assess the truth. Erin cocked her head and considered the dead man in front of her. He hadn’t been in the offices. Not at work. He’d been around the corner at the Harp and Fiddle. He’d been drinking and was getting in his car to drive. He could have killed her on the streets, or worse, some innocent family heading out for dinner. He had been a threat. Erin noticed the details of him. The blond hair. The short nose. The way he was sprawled out toward the cars. Erin’s ears buzzed lightly as muffled thoughts fought for clarity. Something inside pushed past the pain and anger of her attack. Somewhere in the back of her mind, survival made a strange sound not unlike a whimper of resignation. And she knew what to do. He had come from around the elevator bank. He had stepped from the shadows to attack me. He had grabbed me, leering with malicious intent. And I had reacted in self-defense. I didn’t mean to hurt him. I didn’t mean to kill him. I am innocent. Erin silently repeated the created truth several times, a smile eventually overtaking her grimace, knowing they would call her brave. They would reassure her. They would never guess or presume or even suggest it had been anything other than what Erin claimed. After all, why would I kill a perfect stranger? She sat near his feet, away from the growing puddle of blood around his head, and leaned against the cold gray cement wall. Erin reached in her purse for her cellphone as she considered the man, his soiled pants and bloodied face, and noticed which shoe was jutting from the man’s eye. These were the pair she’d been wearing the night of her mugging. But the irony was pushed down inside with her guilt to hide behind the mask she was carefully crafting. While Erin had long ago thrown away the soiled pantsuit, the horrible blue reminder of the attack, she had kept the shoes. She didn’t know why, but it no longer mattered. She was done with them now and didn’t care if they never came back from the evidence locker of the police station. Erin dialed, noticing the blood spatter across her hand and forearm. “911. How can I help you?” “I…” “Ma’am?” Erin tapped into the anxiety she had worked so hard to overcome in therapy. She found the scared version of herself she had spent months healing, the victim with the broken will and cracked voice, and let it come to the surface to play its part. “He… he attacked me.” From the moment I read Chad Lutzke's Of Foster Homes and Flies , I knew he was a writer destined to make his own unique mark on the horror genre, and indeed he has. Praised by such legends as Jack Ketchum and Richard Chizmar, Chad brings his own unique vision of emotional, gut-wrenching horror every time he puts words on the page, as he does with the haunting weird tale, “Womb With a View.”
